Core Description: Sonepur Cattle Fair, also known as Harihar Kshetra Mela, is held at the confluence of the Ganga and Gandak rivers. It is the largest cattle fair in Asia, featuring trade of elephants, horses, cattle, and other animals, along with stalls selling handicrafts, garments, agricultural tools, and household items.
Cultural Significance: Considered both a religious and commercial fair; pilgrims take holy dips and worship at Hariharnath Temple, while the fair preserves Bihar’s ancient trade and cultural heritage

Time/Month: Adhimas (extra lunar month; occurs every 2–3 years)
Core Description: Malmas Mela is held during the Adhimas or Malmas month, which is considered highly sacred in the Hindu calendar. It is believed that all Hindu gods reside in Rajgir during this period, and large religious gatherings, sermons, and continuous worship take place.
Cultural Significance: A major spiritual congregation attracting lakhs of devotees and foreign tourists; strengthens Rajgir’s identity as a sacred religious center

Region/District: Across Bihar (notably riverbanks and pilgrimage sites)
Core Description: Makar Sankranti marks the sun’s transition into Capricorn and the end of winter. People take ritual baths in rivers and ponds, worship Surya, fly kites, and consume seasonal foods such as dahi-chura, tilkut, lai, and khichdi.
Cultural Significance: A harvest-linked festival symbolizing renewal, social bonding, and gratitude; popularly known as the Khichdi festival in Bihar

Core Description: Pitrapaksha Mela is held in Gaya, one of Hinduism’s holiest cities, where devotees perform Shraddha and Pinda Daan rituals for their ancestors. The rites are believed to grant salvation to departed souls.
Cultural Significance: A deeply sacred ancestral observance; reinforces filial duty and makes Gaya a central pilgrimage destination for Hindus

Core Description: Shrawani Mela is a massive pilgrimage during which devotees collect holy water from the Ganga at Sultanganj and walk over 100 km to offer it at the Baba Baidyanath Jyotirlinga in Deoghar. Millions of saffron-clad Kanwariyas participate, chanting ‘Bol Bam’.
Cultural Significance: One of the largest and longest religious fairs in the world; represents intense devotion, endurance, and collective faith

Core Description: Saurath Sabha is an annual matrimonial gathering where Maithil Brahmins assemble to arrange marriages by matching horoscopes through traditional Panjikars. The event takes place in a large orchard donated by the Maharaja of Darbhanga.
Cultural Significance: A unique socio-cultural institution preserving traditional marriage customs and Maithil social organization
